name: Great Globular Cluster in Hercules
description: One of the brightest and best-known globular clusters in the northern sky, containing several hundred thousand stars in a dense spherical halo.

name: Omega Centauri
description: The largest and most massive globular cluster in the Milky Way, suspected to be the remnant nucleus of a dwarf galaxy absorbed by our galaxy.

name: 47 Tucanae
description: The second brightest globular cluster in the sky after Omega Centauri, located in the constellation Tucana near the Small Magellanic Cloud.

name: Cat's Eye Cluster
description: One of the closest globular clusters to Earth, located near Antares in Scorpius. It was the first globular cluster in which individual stars were resolved.

name: Rose Cluster
description: One of the oldest and largest globular clusters, notable for its rich population of RR Lyrae variable stars used as distance indicators.

name: Pinwheel Cluster
description: A large and bright globular cluster in Canes Venatici, containing one of the largest known populations of variable stars among globular clusters.

name: Great Pegasus Cluster
description: A densely concentrated globular cluster in Pegasus, one of the most densely packed clusters known, possibly containing a central black hole.

name: Aquarius Globular
description: A rich and compact globular cluster in Aquarius, one of the larger and brighter globular clusters visible from Earth.

name: Hercules Globular II
description: An ancient globular cluster in Hercules, often overlooked due to nearby M13 but notable as one of the oldest known clusters at roughly 14.2 billion years.

name: Sagittarius Cluster
description: One of the nearest and brightest globular clusters, located toward the galactic center in Sagittarius. One of the first globulars discovered.

name: Pavo Globular
description: The third brightest globular cluster in the sky, located in the constellation Pavo, and one of the nearest globular clusters to the Sun.

name: Scorpius Globular
description: A dense globular cluster in Scorpius, notable for a nova observed within it in 1860, one of only two novae ever seen in a globular cluster.

name: Ophiuchus Globular
description: A globular cluster near the galactic center in Ophiuchus, notable for its asymmetric shape likely caused by tidal interactions with the galactic core.

name: Vela Globular
description: A loose globular cluster in Vela with one of the highest radial velocities of any globular cluster, containing a stellar-mass black hole confirmed by ESO observations.

name: Ara Globular
description: One of the two nearest globular clusters to Earth, having undergone core collapse. Located in the constellation Ara.

name: Ophiuchus Star Cloud
description: A bright globular cluster in Ophiuchus with a moderately concentrated core, lying about 5000 light-years closer to us than the galactic center.

name: Butterfly Globular
description: A loosely concentrated globular cluster in Ophiuchus, notable for having lost many low-mass stars through tidal stripping by the Milky Way.

name: Sagittarius Globular II
description: A compact core-collapsed globular cluster in Sagittarius, located close to the galactic center direction.

name: Carina Globular
description: A massive globular cluster in Carina notable for having three distinct generations of stars, suggesting a complex formation history.

name: Corona Australis Globular
description: A bright and concentrated globular cluster near the border of Corona Australis and Scorpius.

name: Pleiades
description: The most famous open cluster in the sky, easily visible to the naked eye. A young cluster at roughly 100 million years old, embedded in a reflection nebula.

name: Hyades
description: The nearest open cluster to the Sun, forming the V-shape of the constellation Taurus. At 625 million years old, it is a key rung on the cosmic distance ladder.

name: Beehive Cluster
description: A large, bright open cluster in Cancer known since antiquity. Also called Praesepe, it is one of the nearest open clusters to the Solar System.

name: Double Cluster h Persei
description: The western half of the famous Double Cluster in Perseus, a pair of young open clusters visible to the naked eye, only 12.8 million years old.

name: Double Cluster Chi Persei
description: The eastern half of the Double Cluster in Perseus, slightly older and more distant than its companion NGC 869 at roughly 14 million years old.

name: Wild Duck Cluster
description: One of the richest and most compact open clusters known, named for its fan shape resembling a flock of wild ducks in flight. Located in Scutum.

name: Jewel Box Cluster
description: A young open cluster in Crux, named by John Herschel for its colorful mix of blue and red supergiants resembling a casket of jewels.

name: Butterfly Cluster
description: An open cluster in Scorpius whose star arrangement resembles the outline of a butterfly with open wings. Contains the variable orange giant BM Scorpii.

name: Ptolemy Cluster
description: A bright open cluster in Scorpius, recorded by Ptolemy in 130 AD. One of the most prominent open clusters, visible to the naked eye against the Milky Way.

name: Cassiopeia Salt-and-Pepper
description: A rich open cluster in Cassiopeia near the galactic plane, featuring a fan-shaped arrangement of stars. Estimated at 35 million years old.

name: Gemini Cluster
description: A large open cluster in Gemini near the feet of the twins, visible to the naked eye. At 150 million years old, it covers an area the size of the full Moon.

name: Auriga Salt-and-Pepper
description: The richest of the three prominent open clusters in Auriga, containing roughly 500 stars. At 300 million years old, it features many red giants.

name: Southern Pleiades
description: A bright open cluster in Carina visible to the naked eye from southern latitudes, often called the Southern Pleiades for its resemblance to the northern cluster.

name: Wishing Well Cluster
description: A rich open cluster in Carina, described by John Herschel as the finest cluster in the sky. It was the first target observed by the Hubble Space Telescope.

name: Southern Beehive
description: A bright open cluster in Carina visible to the naked eye, sometimes called the Southern Beehive. It contains two bright fifth-magnitude red giants.
